title: Long licenses like GPL are crazy slow
Generate diff of license to known
Is it enterprise grade?
What's legally sufficient to constitute a grant of an open source software license?
body:
From the readme:

> ### Why not just look at the "license" field of [insert package manager here]?
> 
> Because it's not legally binding. A license is a legal contract. You give up certain rights (e.g., the right to sue the author) in exchange for the right to use the software.
> 
> Most popular licenses today _require_ that the license itself be distributed along side the software. Simply putting the letters "MIT" or "GPL" in a configuration file doesn't really meet that requirement.
> 
> Not to mention, it doesn't tell you much about your rights as a user. Is it GPLv2? GPLv2 or later? Those files are designed to be read by computers (who can't enter into contracts), not humans (who can). It's great metadata, but that's about it.
> ### What about looking to see if the author said something in the readme?
> 
> You could make an argument that, when linked or sufficiently identified, the terms of the license are incorporated by reference, or at least that the author's intent is there. There's a handful of reasons why this isn't ideal. For one, if you're using the MIT or BSD (ISC) license, along with a few others, there's templematic language, like the copyright notice, which would go unfilled.
> ### What about checking every single file for a copyright header?
> 
> Because that's silly in the context of how software is developed today. You wouldn't put a copyright notice on each page of a book. Besides, it's a lot of work, as there's no standardized, cross-platform way to describe a project's license within a comment.
> 
> Checking the actual text into version control is definitive, so that's what this project looks at.
## The question

There's a continuum, with the full text of the license being checked into version control on one end, and a non-linked license abbreviation (e.g., "license: MIT") on the other. Which is sufficient to grant a license? Is there any precedence?
1. Full text of the license in version control
2. `LICENSE` file with the license name or abbreviation
3. `README` which references the license name or abbreviation
4. `README` which links to the full license text

title: Make dependency on rugged optional
body: Now that there is support for filesystem-based projects, how about making the dependency on rugged optional, and only use it if it's already installed? The main reason for this being that rugged, as a native extension, can be a pain to install on some platforms, and after all a Git working tree can also be scanned by the filesystem scanner.

title: Recognize non-standard licenses
body:
Uniquely identifying non-standard licenses would make it possible to whitelist them or detect future changes in the text of the licenses.

Currently, repositories such as [github/linguist](https://github.com/github/linguist), which have many licenses in submodules to validate, must use some custom scripts or rely on manual validations. 
Having licensee generate a unique identifier for each non-standard license would make it possible:
- to track any future modification(s) in the license text;
- and to whitelist a few common licenses, such as the [TextMate bundles license](https://github.com/textmate/ant.tmbundle#license).

The unique identifier could eventually be a hash value of the license text.

title: Apache 2.0 notice in README
body:
Some repos eg  https://github.com/jianghejie/XRecyclerView#license https://github.com/glomadrian/loading-balls#license https://github.com/Tibolte/AgendaCalendarView#license include the notice found at http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0#apply in their README, much as the full text of short licenses such as MIT is fairly commonly included in full in READMEs, both often in lieu of a LICENSE file. As we detect (behind a flag) full LICENSE text in READMEs with #68 it _might_ be good to also detect the Apache 2.0 notice in that context.

On the plus side, the Apache 2.0 notice is used verbatim so could easily be detected. On the minus side, verbatim use of the notice really is intended for use in individual source files ("this file").

Alternatively, perhaps licensee should emit a warning that it has detected probable intention of using Apache 2.0 and suggest fix (adding LICENSE)?

title: Use LICENSE.spdx if available
body:
I propose that if there's a file named "LICENSE.spdx" at the top level, this library should use the value of "PackageLicenseDeclared:" (if present) as the license of the package.

This lets people express _exactly_ what the license is using SPDX - so it can handle "or later", multiple licenses, or complex licensing situations.  Since it looks at "PackageLicenseDeclared", it _is_ from the developer, and since SPDX defines what the phrases mean exactly, I think you can assign a lot of confidence to this value.

This would give the licensee library a simple way to handle multiple licenses (per issue #57) with complex situations - just let the developer express it!

Don't get me wrong, I think that people should still include license text in the version control system.  But often that isn't enough.  Detecting the GPL version 2.0 license doesn't tell me if it's "GPL-2.0" (only) or "GPL-2.0+", and once multiple licenses are present, it's tricky to be confident that an automated tool can figure it out.  I think it's _safest_ to depend on a formal spec from the developer, and then use the other mechanisms when that fails.

I realize Ben already knows SPDX, but for readers of this issue who aren't aware of it, here is my [SPDX tutorial](https://github.com/david-a-wheeler/spdx-tutorial).

title: Examples of licensed projects not detected by licensee
body: All of [these projects](https://gist.github.com/bkeepers/8c003ed1aa2da39743ac) on GitHub are not detected by licensee, but there is Good Reason™ to believe that they are actually licensed. There's also a Good Reason™ that most of them aren't detected by licensee, but I thought there might be some patterns in there that we are missing.

title: Add GPLv2+ and GPLv3+ as license options
body:
[Dolphin](https://dolphin-emu.org/blog/2015/05/25/relicensing-dolphin/), [PPSSPP](https://github.com/hrydgard/ppsspp#ppsspp---a-fast-and-portable-psp-emulator), and [MAME](https://github.com/mamedev/mame/blob/master/LICENSE.md), for example, are all using "GPL version 2 or later". These are technically distinct choices from "just" GPLv2, as they allow derivatives to use any future version of the GPL.

This was a problem brought up by the GNU Project, so I thought it was an important consideration to suggest.

From [GPLv2](http://www.gnu.org/licenses/old-licenses/gpl-2.0.en.html):

"9. The Free Software Foundation may publish revised and/or new versions of the General Public License from time to time. Such new versions will be similar in spirit to the present version, but may differ in detail to address new problems or concerns.

Each version is given a distinguishing version number. If the Program specifies a version number of this License which applies to it and "any later version", you have the option of following the terms and conditions either of that version or of any later version published by the Free Software Foundation. If the Program does not specify a version number of this License, you may choose any version ever published by the Free Software Foundation."

From [GPLv3](http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl.html):

"14. Revised Versions of this License.

The Free Software Foundation may publish revised and/or new versions of the GNU General Public License from time to time. Such new versions will be similar in spirit to the present version, but may differ in detail to address new problems or concerns.

Each version is given a distinguishing version number. If the Program specifies that a certain numbered version of the GNU General Public License “or any later version” applies to it, you have the option of following the terms and conditions either of that numbered version or of any later version published by the Free Software Foundation. If the Program does not specify a version number of the GNU General Public License, you may choose any version ever published by the Free Software Foundation.

If the Program specifies that a proxy can decide which future versions of the GNU General Public License can be used, that proxy's public statement of acceptance of a version permanently authorizes you to choose that version for the Program.

Later license versions may give you additional or different permissions. However, no additional obligations are imposed on any author or copyright holder as a result of your choosing to follow a later version."

title: MIT License should be renamed to MIT Expat
body:
Per [the GNU Project's list of Free Software Licenses](http://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.html) there are two licenses known as "The MIT License". The first is the MIT Expat license, and the second is the X11 License. The most common is the MIT Expat, but to prevent confusion they should be distinguished from one another.

I can understand wanting to keep it simple with The MIT License, and I'd be happy to help out in some way to get this working for both use-cases.

body: Do you follow Semantic Versioning http://semver.org? If yes, can this be mentioned in README? If not, can you consider it?

title: Scan more files for licenses (including COPYING)
body:
Based on research at https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/issues/88, I think the method of only looking into the `LICENSE` file is pretty poor as many projects which actually have a license will be overlooked.
Of course, there will probably never a perfect algorithm, but some obvious improvements could be made:
- Scan for the existance of `COPYING`. This file seems is also very commonly used and, to my knowledge, is generally considered to be a valid synonym for `LICENSE`
- Also check for common file name suffix variants: `LICENSE.txt`, `LICENSE.md`, `COPYING.txt`, `COPYING.md`
- Ignore case (just to be safe)

Furthermore, some research should be done on other common ways software specified (e.g. are there other common file names or de facto standard practices which are easily detectible?).

Background info:
The fact that the `COPYING` file is ignored probably means that statistics based on this tool (like this one: https://github.com/blog/1964-open-source-license-usage-on-github-com) are probably WAY too pessimistic since many projects with the `COPYING` files are simply overlooked.
At least, any such statistics should be more carefully worded by claiming that _at least_ X% of the projects have a license, rather than claiming an exact number. I predict that the number of detected licensed projects will “surprisingly” skyrocket by the simple change to also detect `COPYING`. :D

title: Fail detection when appended content is too big
body:
> If we still can't match the license, we use a fancy math thing called the Sørensen–Dice coefficient, which is really good at calculating the similarity between two strings. By calculating the percent changed from the known license to the license file, you can tell, e.g., that a given license is 95% similar to the MIT license, that 5% likely representing legally insignificant changes to the license text.

The issue with this strategy is that if, according to a license, you need to append notices about included/bundled libraries to a LICENSE file, then the appended notice may be too big, sometimes bigger than the actual licence, and cross the threshold.

Examples:
- https://github.com/apache/spark/blob/master/LICENSE
- https://github.com/gradle/gradle/blob/master/LICENSE
- https://github.com/apache/zest-java/blob/develop/LICENSE.txt
- https://github.com/apache/accumulo/blob/master/LICENSE

title: Ability to detect copyright notice without explicit year
body:
How do we distinguish [this line](https://github.com/github/choosealicense.com/blob/gh-pages/_licenses/apache-2.0.txt#L137) from [this line](https://github.com/github/choosealicense.com/blob/gh-pages/_licenses/zlib.txt#L28).

The Zlib license uses a line starting with `(c)` (followed by a date and an arbitrary string) to represent the copyright line.

The Apache license uses a line starting with `(c)` followed by arbitrary text to represent the third bullet of a numbered list.

We had been using the date to distinguish the two. If we treat the 2nd as copyright, I think we're going to get a lot of false positives (Licensee thinks it has a copyright), or false negatives (with that line stripped, license comparison fails).

I think our best bet would be to strip the title (in multiple variants) if it's the start of the license, and the only thing on the line (other than whitespace) and to then assume if the new first line starts with a copyright identifier, that whole line is the copyright.

title: Bug: Licensee can't handle multi-line copyright statements
body:
Licensee fails to determine that our BadgeApp has a bog-standard MIT license.

The reason is that the CII BadgeApp has a 2-line copyright statement (it won't fit on 1 80-column line):
> Copyright (c) 2015-2016 The Linux Foundation,
> the Institute for Defense Analyses, and other project contributors.

The rest of the text is a bog-standard MIT license, but licensee fails to determine its license.  The problem appears to be that licensee strips lines matching a copyright statement, but not the following line if it continues the copyright text.

Sample file which causes the failure is here:
https://github.com/linuxfoundation/cii-best-practices-badge/blob/c23e6c6c88956192266bbf243ba630a6887ef9f2/LICENSE

This wouldn't matter as much if licensee accepted issue #85, which would let people directly specify the actual license in a computer-processable way.  However, since there's no way to provide computer-processable information to licensee, any error or limitation of licensee produces "don't know".  But that's a separate issue; licensee should still be able to handle two-line copyright statements.

Thanks!

The regex defined at https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/blob/cbd96aed7dfd8e91ef780e029e5e6015c3486077/lib/licensee/matchers/copyright_matcher.rb#L9 and used at https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/blob/cbd96aed7dfd8e91ef780e029e5e6015c3486077/lib/licensee/project_files/license_file.rb#L43 matches some text within these licenses.

Few potential ways to fix:

2. Only look for attribution in first part of license text (some reasonable number of lines or characters)
3. Hardcode avoidance of particular matched line above

title: Handling custom licenses
body:
I've been playing with monkeypatching an additional custom license location in. This seems to be working for me:
```
Licensee::License.class_eval do

    def self.add_custom_license_dir(dir)
      @custom_license_dir=dir
    end

    def self.custom_license_dir
      return @custom_license_dir
    end

    def self.license_files
      dir=license_dir()
      files ||= Dir.glob("#{license_dir()}/*.txt")
      files.concat(Dir.glob("#{custom_license_dir()}/*.txt"))
      return files
    end

    def path
       path ||= File.expand_path "#{@key}.txt", Licensee::License.license_dir
       unless(File.exist?(path))
         path = File.expand_path "#{@key}.txt", Licensee::License.custom_license_dir
       end
       return path
    end

end
```
Would you be interested in a patch that makes that more part of the API? I would probably make custom_license_dir an array rather than a single element. Given it has to spend time doing File.exist in path, it could go further and change the path API to return nil if the files don't exist.

title: What should licensee do about an inconsistent license identifier in the README when more definitive license indicators are present?
body:
### Problem

 In [Primer's README](https://github.com/primer/primer-css/tree/80a2eca00b19ba0392d7f5d0955d784eae632725/modules/primer-css#license), it has a `license` heading, with `MIT © GitHub`. 

Prior to v9.x, Licensee had no concept of multiple licenses, and would prefer the LicenseFile over the PackageManagerFile over the ReadmeFIle license section. 

With 9.x, Licensee now identifies each individually, rather than stopping with the first. In the case of Primer, it gets MIT from the license file, MIT from the package manager, and "other" from the README, which means it's a mixed license and returns "other" for `Project#license`.

To note, if the README had the full license text, all three sources would report "MIT", meaning it'd be MIT licensed.

### Solutions

1. Ignore the README heading if other, more definitive sources exist (or put another way, only look at the README if no other sources exist, or only look at the README if its license intent is conclusive). This would work fine for primer, but what if the README said "GPLv3" even though there was MIT license text in the license file or a MIT designation via the package manager. Would a downstream user want Licensee to flag this inconsistency? (I think so).

2. Some sort of fuzzy heuristics on matching the content of the license section of the README with the title / nickname of the suspected license.

3. Continue the current behavior and have downstream consumer migrate to `Project#license`**s** and let them make their own determination / flag it for the user etc. (cc @bkeepers, @jonabc)

4. Drop README support entirely.

Thoughts?

body:
Test case:

```ruby

Running this against `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pgbouncer/pgbouncer/master/COPYRIGHT`, for example, yields `other`. title: Apache License 2.0 isn't detected when applied according to their instructions
body:
The [Apache License, Version 2.0](https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0) contains an appendix, that contains a boilerplate notice that contains the following line: 

```
Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License"); ...
```

The GitHub repository [wiremod/wire](https://github.com/wiremod/wire) contains the text of the Apache license in the file `license`, and the boilerplate notice verbatim in `readme.md`. This results in the following:

```
$ licensee
License: Other
Matched files: ["license", "readme.md"]
license:
  Content hash: d7bc6f56cd49606d0134945d4b91ae47d3cee458
  Confidence: 100.00%
  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Exact
  License: Apache License 2.0
readme.md:
  Content hash: a953ac3d253aaf14bf9a2d62f780811dd8b4f26e
  Attribution: Copyright 2009-2017 Wire Team
  License: Other
```

The issue here is that `readme.md` doesn't mention the phrase "Apache License 2.0", only "Apache License, Version 2.0" (which is the proper name as specified by the Apache License itself). When "Apache License, Version 2.0" is changed to "Apache License 2.0" the correct result is returned:

```
$ sed -i 's/Apache License, Version 2.0/Apache License 2.0/' readme.md
$ git add readme.md && git commit -m 'temp' --quiet
$ licensee
License: Apache License 2.0
Matched files: ["license", "readme.md"]
license:
  Content hash: d7bc6f56cd49606d0134945d4b91ae47d3cee458
  Confidence: 100.00%
  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Exact
  License: Apache License 2.0
readme.md:
  Content hash: f4f083f8593f4451b48836075245d53ee12e2976
  Attribution: Copyright 2009-2017 Wire Team
  Confidence: 90.00%
  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Reference
  License: Apache License 2.0
```

I think it makes sense to consider this a bug in Licensee (or choosealicense.com's vendor files), as applying the license using Apache's own boilerplate causes the license to be incorrectly detected.

```
$ gem list licensee

*** LOCAL GEMS ***

licensee (9.5.0)
```

title: Disappointing false negatives and dangerous false positives
body:
Without knowing that this is the gem used by GitHub themselves I posted a reddit thread [here](https://www.reddit.com/r/github/comments/7c84sx/a_social_hacking_vulnerability_with_license_info/).

In short
* It's disappoiting that projects with short licenses (eg MIT) and many authors (or even a single author with a long name) aren't matched by the algorithm just because their names overshoot the 5%
* It's dangerous to assume that those 5% represent insignificant changes to the license. It is conceivable that 5% (especially for a longer license) can be used maliciously to add or change clauses

title: Return SPDX license expressions?
body:
On Mon, Nov 27, 2017 at 04:10:25PM +0000, Ben Balter [wrote][1]:
>     +  "license": "(MIT OR Apache-2.0 OR AGPL-3.0+)",
>
> It will take some reworking of the logic in the Project class, but in a subsequent iteration, it'd be cool to be able to parse the expression and return multiple licenses, where we can match the SPDX ID.

This also came up in #241:

> Other because licensee doesn't know about `CC-BY-3.0` (only 4.0), nor about `+`.

I'd expect Licensee to return [SPDX license expressions][2] (not just license IDs).  In that case, `package.json` processing would remove/process [npm extentions][3] like `SEE LICENSE IN <filename>` and `UNLICENSED` (although see spdx/spdx-spec#49 about maybe addressing the `UNLICENSED` use case from within license expressions) but would return SPDX license expressions unmodified.  And `LICENSE.spdx` processors could return the declared license expression directly too.

Parsing license expressions might be useful for cases like “`package.json` declares `GPL-2.0` but `COPYING` contains `GPL-3.0`”.  But if `package.json` declares `GPL-2.0+` and `COPYING` contains `GPL-2.0`, I think Licensee should be returning the full `GPL-2.0+`.

[1]: https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/pull/240#pullrequestreview-79219233
[2]: https://spdx.org/spdx-specification-21-web-version#h.jxpfx0ykyb60
[3]: https://docs.npmjs.com/files/package.json#license

The issue is that GitHub/licensee doesn't detect the license for that repo. This is the output of `licensee`:

	License: Other
	Matched files: ["LICENSE", "COPYRIGHT"]
	LICENSE:
	  Content hash: f9aaff304edbbe732f27b554dcb6abe49e0b4fa0
	  Attribution: Copyright (C) 2007 Free Software Foundation, Inc. <https://fsf.org/>
	  Confidence: 100.00%
	  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Exact
	  License: GNU General Public License v3.0
	COPYRIGHT:
	  Content hash: b55b124b3b2c9d14da5e071b1b080dfcd78e1250
	  Attribution: Copyright 2016-2017 The Board of Trustees of the Leland Stanford Junior University,
	  License: Other

I realized that the problem is our multi-line [COPYRIGHT file](https://github.com/lsst/daf_butler/blob/master/COPYRIGHT):

	Copyright 2016-2017 The Board of Trustees of the Leland Stanford Junior University,
	through SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory
	Copyright 2018 Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy
	Copyright 2015, 2018 The Trustees of Princeton University

`licensee` detects just the first copyright line, and then interprets the rest of the text as an unknown license.

If I modify the license to be just a single line (no newlines), like this:

	Copyright 2016-2017 The Board of Trustees of the Leland Stanford Junior University, through SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory. Copyright 2018 Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy. Copyright 2015, 2018 The Trustees of Princeton University

Then `licensee` understands that `COPYRIGHT` is only a copyright statement and confidently understands the overall licensing:

	License: GNU General Public License v3.0
	Matched files: ["LICENSE", "COPYRIGHT"]
	LICENSE:
	  Content hash: f9aaff304edbbe732f27b554dcb6abe49e0b4fa0
	  Attribution: Copyright (C) 2007 Free Software Foundation, Inc. <https://fsf.org/>
	  Confidence: 100.00%
	  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Exact
	  License: GNU General Public License v3.0
	COPYRIGHT:
	  Content hash: da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709
	  Attribution: Copyright 2016-2017 The Board of Trustees of the Leland Stanford Junior University, through SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory. Copyright 2018 Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy. Copyright 2015, 2018 The Trustees of Princeton University
	  Confidence: 100.00%
	  Matcher: Licensee::Matchers::Copyright
	  License: No-license

In my org, partner institutions contribute code to the project. Their contracts allow each institution to maintain copyright on their contributions. This is why we track multiple copyrights. Our format is that each institution gets its own line in the `COPYRIGHT` file, with the `COPYRIGHT <years> <name>` format.

To support this, I think that ideally, `licensee` would identify a paragraph starting with the word COPYRIGHT and then allow the whole paragraph to be considered the copyright statement. This behavior is also good for folks that apply hard-wrapping to their copyright statements.

What do you think? I'd add a PR myself but I don't know Ruby.

```
$ script/git-repo https://github.com/lsst/daf_butler
License:        GPL-3.0
Matched files:  LICENSE, COPYRIGHT
LICENSE:
  Content hash:  f9aaff304edbbe732f27b554dcb6abe49e0b4fa0

    MIT similarity:      65.10%
    BSL-1.0 similarity:  52.36%
    ISC similarity:      52.17%
```

1. `COPYING.BSD2` is the NetBSD BSD-2 license noted in #323 (which I have now amended to use the generic "COPYRIGHT HOLDER" instead of NetBSD).
2. `COPYING.UCD` is the Unicode Data Files and Software license (issue #326).
3. `COPYING.IEEE` is the declaration from http://www.realitypixels.com/turk/opensource/ for http://www.realitypixels.com/turk/opensource/#IEEEIO (no separate issue).

The project is GPL-3 licensed as a whole, so it would be useful for licensee to reflect that fact, as well as other combinations for other project license combinations.

It may also be useful to attach a confidence on the overall license like is done with matching the individual licenses.

title: Support for debian's machine-readable copyright file format
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

Currently, licensee does not understand debian's machine-readable copyright file format: https://www.debian.org/doc/packaging-manuals/copyright-format/1.0/. This can lead to a `NOASSERTION` decision by licensee.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Licensee should recognize copyright files formatted with debian's format by applying its standard detection mechanism on each mentioned/referenced license in such a file. It should then add them as input to its standard decision mechanism on the main license as if they were found in seperate files.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

None.

### Additional context

I would like to propose a PR for this feature myself. But before I invest the time, I would like to check in with you first, if you would even consider accepting such a feature.

title: Add support for `/docs/LICENSE.md`
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

When GitHub Pages introduced the [/docs](https://help.github.com/articles/configuring-a-publishing-source-for-github-pages/#publishing-your-github-pages-site-from-a-docs-folder-on-your-master-branch) folder, we moved `/LICENSE.md` to `/docs/LICENSE.md` so it would render on the [website](https://marked.js.org/#/LICENSE.md).

Now it appears that GitHub no longer sees the license (maybe it never did though).

### Describe the solution you'd like

If the license was not found in the root, check the `/docs` folder.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

An alternative is having two copies of this file, one in root and one in docs but I like to keep things [DRY](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don%27t_repeat_yourself).

Another alternative is to change our website to read the license directly from github instead of the publishes `/docs` folder. Possible but maybe not desirable.

### Additional context

Someone noticed this here: https://github.com/markedjs/marked/pull/1343

title: BSD 3-clause license not detected
body:
### Describe the bug

A valid variant of the BSD 3-clause license is not detected. Licensee does not detect https://github.com/jermar/helenos/blob/master/LICENSE.md as a valid BSD 3-clause license. Note that this version of the BSD 3-clause uses the wording from https://directory.fsf.org/wiki/License:BSD-3-Clause. Another minor difference is the use of bullet points / dashes instead of numbered bullets. When experimenting with this, I found that the problem seems to be the former, i.e. the use of "the author" instead of "the name of the copyright holder nor the names of its contributors" in the list of conditions and "THE COPYRIGHT HOLDERS AND CONTRIBUTORS" in the disclaimer part. According to FSF and also SPDX, such a license is still a BSD 3-clause license.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. Go to https://github.com/jermar/helenos
2. Click on 'View license'
3. See error, i.e. LICENSE.md is not detected as a BSD 3-clause license

### Expected behavior

According to the SPDX matching rules, even this is a BSD 3-clause license. And the wording corresponds to the FSF wording for the BSD 3-clause license.

title: Add git repo ref argument to GithubProject#load_file
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

licensee is awesome - thank you! It would be even better if it were possible to specify a ref version when fetching a license from a remote GitHub repo. As-is, it's a bit unreliable/unsafe from a legal perspective to fetch the license in the latest commit when that might be inconsistent with the version of the dependency you are using.

### Describe the solution you'd like

licensee commands which accept the `[PATH]` argument should also accept a `--ref` argument which is a string to be processed by the GitHub API.

Note that [refs can be passed to the GitHub contents API](https://github.com/octokit/octokit.rb#additional-query-parameters):

```rb
 client.contents('octokit/octokit.rb', path: 'path/to/file.rb', query: {ref: 'some-other-branch'})
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

One alternative is to do licensees's work manually and make several requests to the GitHub contents API for different license file name strings. At that rate, we should just be merging a feature into licensee, I think.

### Additional context

This is especially useful for dependencies which are developed in monorepos, such as the popular npm libraries React, Jest and Babel. They have many sub-packages which are distributed individually on npm and in some cases the library authors forget to include the actual license text in the package, even though it is available at the root of the repository on GitHub. In order to ensure we fetch the proper version, we want to specify a tag ref which can be derived from the version string.

title: Incorrect yaml output for multiline attribution string
body:
### Describe the bug

When an attribution string contains multiple lines (an example is the `accepts` module from npm, version 1.3.3), the output doesn't come out as correct yaml. For `accepts`, we get this output:

```yaml
License:        MIT
Matched files:  LICENSE
LICENSE:
  Content hash:  d64f3bb4282a97b37454b5bb96a8a264a3363dc3
  Attribution:   Copyright (c) 2014 Jonathan Ong <me@jongleberry.com>
Copyright (c) 2015 Douglas Christopher Wilson <doug@somethingdoug.com>
  Confidence:    100.00%
  Matcher:       Licensee::Matchers::Exact
  License:       MIT
```

Yaml parsers (such as `js-yaml` from npm) don't know how to parse this and I get this error:

```
(node:18392) UnhandledPromiseRejectionWarning: Unhandled promise rejection (rejection id: 1): YAMLException: can not read a block mapping entry; a multiline key may not be an implicit key at line 7, column 13:
      Confidence:    100.00%
```

I consulted [this guide](https://yaml-multiline.info/) to handling multiline strings in yaml and I found this is the correct output format:

```yaml
License:        MIT
Matched files:  LICENSE
LICENSE:
  Content hash:  d64f3bb4282a97b37454b5bb96a8a264a3363dc3
  Attribution:   "Copyright (c) 2014 Jonathan Ong <me@jongleberry.com>\\nCopyright (c) 2015 Douglas Christopher Wilson <doug@somethingdoug.com>"
  Confidence:    100.00%
  Matcher:       Licensee::Matchers::Exact
  License:       MIT
```

As you can see we surround the multiline string in double quotes, and instead of a literal newline we use `\\n` (regular `\n` is ignored). Also we would clearly need to escape any double quotes that appear in the attribution string, if there were any.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. `licensee detect --remote --no-packages --no-readme jshttp/accepts`
2. You should see the same poorly formed yaml string as I printed above

### Expected behavior

Yaml I can parse appropriately

title: Recognize REUSE Initiative markup in LICENSE files?
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

I read that GitHub is using this gem to detect the license of each repository. If I have a `LICENSE` file in my repo with a verbatim copy of e.g. the BSD-2-Clause license, then it correctly shows up as `BSD-2-Clause`. However, if I add the following markup at the beginning of that `LICENSE` file:

    Valid-License-Identifier: BSD-2-Clause
    License-Text:

    <...full text of the BSD-2-Clause license here, as usual...>

then it's detected as an unknown license.

This markup is used by the [REUSE Initiative](https://reuse.software/practices/2.0/) so that their software tools can determine what license is in question. The current situation is that I can have either GitHub or the REUSE tool determine the correct license, but not both at the same time :) The REUSE conventions require the markup in the `LICENSE` file even for well-known licenses.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Would it be possible to add support for the REUSE markup to *licensee*?

It could simply do a regexp or string match for the markup at the beginning of each `LICENSE ` file, perhaps after normalizing  whitespace. There ought to be few (if any) variations in how the markup is written. Luckily, the `Valid-License-Identifier`s they use are the exact same ones as the `SPDX-License-Identifier`s that *licensee* is already using.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I'm not using *licensee* for anything other than convenience, so this is not critical.

### Additional context

Here's [an example repo of mine](https://github.com/lassik/go-httplog)

title: Spurious files treated as potential license files eg check-licenses.sh
body:
Example repo with a `*-licenses.*` file that is source code, not a potential license file https://github.com/iheanyi/licensed-action

```
$ script/git-repo https://github.com/iheanyi/licensed-action
License:        NOASSERTION
Matched files:  check-licenses.sh, package.json
check-licenses.sh:
  Content hash:  87265e53c2ccd3456ffc5b9c500713e438e9ec11
  License:       NOASSERTION
package.json:
  Confidence:  90.00%
  Matcher:     Licensee::Matchers::NpmBower
  License:     MIT
```

Instead check-licenses.sh should be ignored by licensee.

This behavior is caused by an extremely liberal regex, which matches any extension except a few that have been excluded because someone has noted a spurious treatment as a license file in the past:

https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/blob/461041383a9e59cf180c946938f1def1c7214241/lib/licensee/project_files/license_file.rb#L11

I suspect it would make sense to change `OTHER_EXT_REGEX` to only match a handful of text-like extensions besides those already in 

https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/blob/461041383a9e59cf180c946938f1def1c7214241/lib/licensee/project_files/license_file.rb#L7-L8

Or even remove `OTHER_EXT_REGEX` and just append more to the above?

Likely additions: text, rst, asciidoc, textile, htm[l], LICENSE_REGEX, COPYING_REGEX?

Some of those, well at least html, aren't going to result in positive license identifications by licensee, but are plausible license files so shouldn't be ignored.

But `license.py`, `check-licenses.sh`, etc etc ought be ignored.

title: Minor license revisions in GPL 3.0 cause license misidentification
body:
### Describe the bug

GPL 3.0 license changed slightly without updating the license version, as you can see in:

* [old] https://web.archive.org/web/20170914030415/http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0.txt
* [new] https://web.archive.org/web/20170930072152/http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-3.0.txt

Licensee's GPL 3.0 file was updated accordingly in:

* https://github.com/licensee/licensee/pull/317/files#diff-cb5cb76367ec37937600f38e3193acb1

However, this means that previous versions of GPL 3.0 are no longer detected, forcing all projects to update to the new GPL 3.0 version to be detected correctly, or have their license displayed as "View license" which suggests they have a non-standard or proprietary license, when in fact, they are bona fide open-source projects.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

As a result of the above change, projects such as [Mosh][mosh] which adopted the GPL 3.0 license with the [old] version above are not displaying "GPL 3.0" but "View license" instead (see screenshot below).

### Expected behavior

It would be preferable to detect all minor revisions of GPL 3.0 separately, not just the latest one, so that all projects don't have to keep updating their LICENSE file just to get their license identified correctly.

### Screenshots

E.g., [Mosh][mosh] displays the following:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4074659/60819973-83218f80-a16e-11e9-8a0b-6d19a4e84459.png)

> Thanks to @andersk for the research & references above in https://github.com/mobile-shell/mosh/pull/1050#issuecomment-508344199

[mosh]: https://github.com/mobile-shell/mosh

title: Apache 2.0 Licence not properly detected
body:
This is the project GitHub uses to detect what license projects use, right? If not, please disregard and close this issue.

Our project (https://github.com/robotframework/robotframework) uses the Apache 2.0 license and our LICENSE.txt file has been copied directly from http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt. Oddly enough this license isn't properly detected and the banner in our project page just says "View license".

I found this strange as, for example, Apache 2.0 License used by https://github.com/cssjanus/cssjanus (noticed this project when searching related issues here) is detected fine. I compared their LICENSE.txt to the original from apache.org and noticed these differences:

1. Header in their version is a bit different. It misses the leading empty line and the first content line is aligned wrong. I doubt this matters.

2. Their version has `enclosed by brackets "{}"` but the original has `enclosed by brackets "[]"`.  This change looks strange but I doubt  it matters.

3. They have changed "Copyright [yyyy] [name of copyright owner]" in the appendix to contain their actual copyright information. My understanding has been that this appendix is there to instruct how to apply the license in general and shouldn't be updated like this. We follow the convention explained in the appendix in our source files and also have a separate COPYRIGHT.txt file at the root of our repository. Anyway, I would assume this is the difference that matters.

Questions:

1. Have I understood it wrong and the appendix in LICENSE.txt should actually be updated to contain our copyright information?

2. Even if I have, isn't it pretty obvious that we use Apache 2.0 License when our LICENSE.txt is 100% identical to the original available at http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt?

3. The appendix is after the explicit "END OF TERMS AND CONDITIONS" line. Why is such information even processed?

I'd like GitHub to recognize my project as MIT licensed, but instead Licensee categorizes my project as "other"

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'd like Licensee to recognize that of the multiple licenses supported by my project, one of them is a recognized license, and use that as the matched license

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I could get what I want by renaming the `LICENSE-BlueOak.md` file to `LICENSE2-BlueOak.md`, so that the only file whose name starts with `LICENSE-` will be `LICENSE-MIT`. But trying to hide information from Licensee doesn't sound like the right approach, what if it the regexes change to recognize `LICENSE<number>` in the future? Licensee is already supposed to handle multi-licensed projects.

### Additional context

Even when you [view the `LICENSE-MIT` file on GitHub](https://github.com/laughinghan/mechanical/blob/master/LICENSE-MIT), it still doesn't show that it's recognized, even though I copied the text verbatim from GitHub's own https://choosealicense.com:

![Screen Shot 2020-03-12 at 14 23 28](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/225809/76571866-d07c9c00-6475-11ea-8fd1-c16e50bfdfec.png)

Contrast:

title: Detection when both LICENSE and COPYING file are present
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

Some GPL-licensed repositores are using both LICENSE and COPYING files. In that case, COPYING contains full license text (for example full GPLv3 text), while LICENSE contains short license headers, notices in case of using *or later* license, and licenses of dependencies.

### Describe the solution you'd like

* For example, [GIMP](https://github.com/GNOME/gimp) is licensed under GPLV3 or later and is using [COPYING](https://github.com/GNOME/gimp/blob/mainline/COPYING) file for standard license text, while [LICENSE](https://github.com/GNOME/gimp/blob/mainline/LICENSE) file contains short license notice as well as notice for the license of `libgimp` and other libraries. In this case, Licensee detects COPYING file as GPLv3 while LICENSE as NOASSERTION, so the whole project is detected as NOASSERTION.

  In this case, as LICENSE file doesn't have any particular format, it would probably be hard to detect it as GPLv3 or later, but it should still be able to detect repository as GPLv3 because of COPYING.

* Another example, [ZeroNet](https://github.com/HelloZeroNet/ZeroNet), is using GPLv3 and similar file structure. However, in this case, LICENSE file is using GNU's standard license header, so Licensee should detect it as GPLv3.

* In case if project is using a similar structure with standard header and GPLv3 *or later* license, this is often also specified in LICENSE file so it should be detected.

title: Tag for third-party / linked components after main license
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

There are many repositories on GitHub that fail to be detected by `licensee` / GitHub given the additional information about the license of third-party / linked components. For example, [JuliaLang/julia](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/LICENSE.md) (MIT) or [ericphanson/UnbalancedOptimalTransport.jl](https://github.com/ericphanson/UnbalancedOptimalTransport.jl/blob/master/LICENSE) (MIT). Other repositories are a bit harder (e.g., [postgres/postgres](https://github.com/postgres/postgres)).

### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be great to have a public facing best practices in those scenarios to make the license machine detectable. For example, GitHub Choosealicense has material for choosing a license and how to put it in your repository but it does not cover cases such as third-party components, etc.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Would be good to have a standard way of expressing that syntax such as `# Additional Components` or something or something like standardizing a header before the license with the `spdxID` or something.

### Additional context

Would love to hear what @mlinksva and @benbalter think it would be best practices for developers to adhere for "universal" machine-detectable.

title: Reporting deliberatly `UNLICENSED` license selection
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

Licensee currently reports `NOASSERTION` for `package.json` files (at least) which use the `UNLICENSED` or `SEE LICENSE IN <filename>` patterns. This is typically used when there is deliberately no license being provided (vs. "no assertion" being made).

### Describe the solution you'd like

If `UNLICENSED` (and ideally `SEE LICENSE IN`) are detected in as the `license` value, those values should be reported by Licensee and _not_ the current `NOASSERTION`--which is technically incorrect.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Leaving things as they are is probably the only other alternative available, and it sends the wrong message to the person running Licensee as jurisdictions differ on the implicit license given by making content publicly available without any license assertion--vs. explicitly saying that no license is provided (i.e. it's unlicensed) or that a less well known license is provided in a specific file.

### Additional context

Licensee is part of [repolinter](https://github.com/todogroup/repolinter/) and [licensed](https://github.com/github/licensed/) and many other tools for copyright/license code scanning (which is awesome! 😁). I'm currently putting tooling in place for enforcing open source usage policies at the company I work for and in my side-projects, and "accidentally" open sourcing something can be a real risk (especially at "scale"--i.e. lots of less informed developers).

This change would make it much more obvious (to the robots) that something should not be open sourced or (in the positive cases) that a deliberate action MUST be taken prior to its being open sourced.

`npm init` defaults to `ISC`, so accidentally tagged internal-only JS repos are no uncommon, but putting policy in place (backed by Licensee via repolinter) is currently hampered by this reporting bug.

I'm happy to send in a patch, but could use a pointer as to where best to put it. 😺 

Thanks for building this tool!
🎩

title: Command-line option to match a given file
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

I have a project to compile reports of all 3rd party dependency licenses. I'm able to extract all of the license files/names/URLs etc, but as of now, the only way to get the tool to detect the license of that file is by putting it into its own directory and naming it `LICENSE`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Currently `licensee detect [PATH]` works if the path is a directory containing a LICENSE (etc) file, or if the path **is** directly to a file named LICENSE.

The proposed solution would treat a case where `[PATH]` points a single file, the same as if it had been discovered as a LICENSE file. For example:

```
$ ls output/deps/
com.example.artifact1.lic
com.github.artifact2.lic
$ licensee detect output/deps/com.example.artifact1.lic
License:        Apache-2.0
Matched files:  output/deps/com.example.artifact1.lic
LICENSE:
  Content hash:  ab3901051663cb8ee5dea9ebdff406ad136910e3
  Confidence:    100.00%
  Matcher:       Licensee::Matchers::Exact
  License:       Apache-2.0
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Current workaround is to create a separate directory for every dependency, and place the output into a single file named `LICENSE`, and then run the detection against that file or directory.  Using above example:

```
$ mkdir output/deps/com.example.artifact1/
$ mv output/deps/com.example.artifact1.lic output/deps/com.example.artifact1/LICENSE
$ licensee detect output/deps/com.example.artifact1/          # WORKS
$ licensee detect output/deps/com.example.artifact1/LICENSE   # Also WORKS
```

This works, but significantly changes the structure and requirements, for what appears to be arbitrary reasons.

title: Treat URLs to known licenses the same as full text license
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

If a LICENSE file or similar entry consists solely of a link to a well known license URL (such as `http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt`), this is currently not detected as the "Apache-2.0" license.

Some dependencies declare their license intent by just a plain URL to the license (whether it's a proprietary license or a well known open source license). Currently a file with this content is detected as `None` or `NOASSERTION`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A license described only by its URL, at least for well-known license URLs like this, should be interpreted as the associated license.  Such that `http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt` should be interpreted as `Apache-2.0` license.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

One workaround could be to detect if the contents of the file consist only of a URL, and then download that URL into a LICENSE file in order to match against it. But matching against a URL is much less text to sift through, compared to the full text of the license.

### Additional context

This particular project is using the [Google Open Source Licenses plugin for Gradle](https://developers.google.com/android/guides/opensource) in order to extract license information from each declared Gradle dependency. That content is then written to a seek-file with separate offset/length metadata. The contents can be extracted from the seek file by reading the start/length range from the file, for each dependency.  Some of those extracted licenses are full text (in some cases, the full Apache-2 license, or the Android SDK license, etc); but many of those are declared using only the license URL -- some of which are links to popular licenses like Apache-2.0, though some are considered proprietary licenses pointing to that company's terms of services.

Examples:
* Dagger (`com.google.dagger:dagger`) dependency extracts the full Apache-2.0 license text
* Kotlin Coroutines (`org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-coroutines-core`) extracts only the URL: `http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt`
* Fabric plugin (`io.fabric.sdk.android:fabric`) extracts a URL to the company's terms: `https://fabric.io/terms`

The [kotlinx-coroutines POM](https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/org/jetbrains/kotlinx/kotlinx-coroutines-core/1.3.5/kotlinx-coroutines-core-1.3.5.pom) defines the license as:
```xml
  <licenses>
    <license>
      <name>The Apache Software License, Version 2.0</name>
      <url>https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt</url>
      <distribution>repo</distribution>
    </license>
  </licenses>
```
But the Google `oss-licenses-plugin` extracts only the URL from this.

title: Regression: Artistic License 2.0 (Markdown) no longer detected
body:
### Describe the bug

As of [v9.11.0](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/releases/tag/v9.11.0) (Jan 2019), Licensee no longer detects [Artistic License 2.0](https://opensource.org/licenses/artistic-license-2.0) files (Markdown). They were detected correctly prior to [this commit](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/commit/d01660a751977271829339a3d57a181cbac76158).

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. Download an Artistic License 2.0 file in Markdown format, [e.g.](https://github.com/chocolateboy/wireless/blob/master/LICENSE.md)
2. Run the test script (see below)

#### Expected

```
ok: Artistic License 2.0
```

#### Actual

```
error: "Other"
```

### Additional context

## test.rb

```ruby
#!/usr/bin/ruby -W0

require 'licensee'

LICENSE = ARGV.shift || 'LICENSE.md'
WANT = 'Artistic License 2.0'

license = Licensee.license(LICENSE)&.name

if license == WANT
  puts "ok: #{license}"
else
  puts "error: #{license.inspect}"
end
```

title: Projects with modified Apache license are marked as Apache
body:
### Describe the bug

Projects that use a modified Apache license are being marked as Apache.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

See https://github.com/PixarAnimationStudios/ruby-jss or https://github.com/PixarAnimationStudios/chook
Click on the license. It is a modified Apache license.

Note the license indicator on the project is listed as "Apache".
### Expected behavior

Any project that *modifies* clauses of the Apache license should be marked as non-standard. They may or may not be open source and the change may or may not matter. The fact is, however, nobody can trust an automated decision about that. Once you modify the license, you have to get your lawyer involved to understand the change.

title: LICENSE is not detected for repository when other files named `license-checks.xml` and `license_test.go` exist
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.
Problem: Repository License is not being reliably detected in a file named LICENSE in cases where other files named license exist.

Organizations with large numbers of open source repositories such as [googleapis](https://github.com/googleapis) have placed their license in a file named `LICENSE` at the root of their repository. 

https://docs.github.com/en/github/creating-cloning-and-archiving-repositories/licensing-a-repository

### Describe the solution you'd like
Have a check which finds LICENSE (all uppercase) and if found does not evaluate further files or introduced them for making a decision about the type of license.

### Additional context

 Renaming or removing files named `license_test.go`, `license-checks.xml` is not a practical solution for large numbers of open source reposotories where organizations want to have their open source software's license be detectable by the GitHub license API.

title: REUSE 3.0 Specification Support?
body:
Hello, I am opening a new issue in the same spirit as #413

I think it would be really handy if licensee could detect a license if the [REUSE 3.0 Specification](https://reuse.software/spec/) is in use. Specifically, REUSE dictates that all license files are located in the `LICENSES/` directory at the root of a project, even if there is only one license.

I personally think there's a simple implementation to detect if there's only one file in this directory, and if there is, declare it to be the project's license. If not, then it's clear something more complicated is happening with the project's license, and it's worth it to declare the project license as unknown. A human likely has to intervene anyway in these more complex cases, even if licensee could semantically detect a multiple license scenario correctly.

Thank you for all the work you've done on licensee, and please feel free to close this issue out if this feature request continues to be a `wontfix`. Thank you!

title: Error when specifying a bundler gem from a git repo
body:
### Describe the bug

I'm not totally sure if this is an issue with this library or should be filed at https://github.com/github/licensed due to how it uses this library, but when using a git repo for a bundler gem, licensee reports the following error:
```
Search root must be the project path directory or its ancestor
from /Users/john/.rbenv/versions/3.0.1/lib/ruby/gems/3.0.0/gems/licensed-3.0.0/lib/licensed/dependency.rb:40:in `initialize'
        from /Users/john/.rbenv/versions/3.0.1/lib/ruby/gems/3.0.0/gems/licensed-3.0.0/lib/licensed/sources/bundler.rb:17:in `initialize'
        from /Users/john/.rbenv/versions/3.0.1/lib/ruby/gems/3.0.0/gems/licensed-3.0.0/lib/licensed/sources/bundler.rb:60:in `new'
        from /Users/john/.rbenv/versions/3.0.1/lib/ruby/gems/3.0.0/gems/licensed-3.0.0/lib/licensed/sources/bundler.rb:60:in `block (2 levels) in enumerate_dependencies'

(this is used in conjunction with licensed)

My gemfile specifies:
```
gem 'active_admin_import', '>= 4.2.1.ruby3', git: 'https://github.com/Shelf-Life/active_admin_import'

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

2. Run `bundle install`
3. Run `gem install licensed`

`licensed` should run as expected with no errors.

title: Support Asciidoc formatting of LICENSE files (LICENSE.adoc)
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

I would like GitHub to recognize LICENSE files in ASCIIDOC format (LICENSE.adoc).

### Describe the solution you'd like

While GitHub has extended their integrated files support to include ASCIIDOC much as they've done with MarkDown and others, this integration doesn't include support in the LICENSE detection.  This appears to be a limitation in the licensee gem.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Use leave in plain text or migrate to MD.

### Additional context

This is a "suggestion" not a hard requirements.  I'm just wondering if you've considered this and have any plans to add this support in the next 3-6 months.  (I also suspect this is a slippery slope issue as the number of formats will only grow.)

I've checked the closed issues and don't see any discussion of this idea.

Sadly, I'm not a Ruby expert and thus proposing a patch would be a larger learning curve than time permits me at this point.

Thanks in advance for your patience with answering what can be viewed as a silly question.

title: Common gems (activerecord, pg) not being detected properly (GitHub action environment)
body:
### Describe the bug

Common gems are labeled `other` instead of `mit`.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

Using the https://github.com/github/licensed gem, but understood that this is the library that is actually doing the license detection.

Using a standard Rails app (6.1.4.4), running the following commands in a GitHub action environment:
```
bundle exec licensed cache
bundle exec licensed status
```

Results in a lot of cache entries labeled `other` instead of i.e. `mit`. Some examples:

```
Errors:
* app.bundler.activerecord
  filename: /app/.licenses/bundler/activerecord.dep.yml, version: 6.1.4.4, license: other, allowed: false
    - license needs review: other

* app.bundler.bcrypt
  filename: /app/.licenses/bundler/bcrypt.dep.yml, version: 3.1.16, license: other, allowed: false
    - license needs review: other

* app.bundler.elasticsearch
  filename: /app/.licenses/bundler/elasticsearch.dep.yml, version: 7.6.0, license: other, allowed: false
    - license needs review: other

* app.bundler.pg
  filename: /app/.licenses/bundler/pg.dep.yml, version: 1.3.1, license: other, allowed: false
    - license needs review: other
```

### Expected behavior

I would expect `activerecord` to be labeled `mit`, same as `activemodel`, `activejob` etc. The cache entries themselves look *very* similar, not sure why `activerecord` gets mislabeled.

### Additional context

Verified on multiple local systems and also in a GitHub action.

title: Suggestion: Vendor the latest tag from the SPDX license list rather than the latest commit
body:
### Describe the bug

More of a suggestion to consider rather than a bug.

The SPDX license list main branch is updated with in-progress changes.  Prior to release, all of the changes are reviewed by the SPDX legal team.  There are (somewhat rare) situations where a license XML file may be changed after the initial commit and prior to a release.  Released versions are considered more stable than the in-progress main branch.

Releases are tagged with the release version.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

This is based on a review of the [vendor-spdx file](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/blob/master/script/vendor-spdx).  It appears to pull the latest from main.

### Expected behavior

Pull from the latest tag.

### Additional information

This [stack-overflow article](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1404796/how-can-i-get-the-latest-tag-name-in-current-branch-in-git) provides a suggestion on how to identify the latest tag.

title: Multiple alternative licenses and SPDX identifiers
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

Hello, I would like GitHub to properly detect licenses of software like @Nette (e.g. [nette/schema license](https://github.com/nette/schema/blob/469e67c54f64f23b21a1d8f1c95400ddcd689968/license.md).

### Describe the solution you'd like

Support [SPDX license identifiers](https://spdx.org/licenses/) so we could cleary specify in license file supported licenses and whether they are combined with AND or OR

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently working way (supported by GitHub) is adding multiple license files (e.g. license.bsd-3.md, license.gpl3.md) with content matching general text of these licenses. But it is unclear whether these licenses should by combined by AND or OR as it is not documented, it is unclear whether and how other tools for checking licenses and their compatibility support it and I am not aware there is a way to express "GPL-2.0 or newer version" etc.

### Additional context

SPDX would help with cross-compatibility, deterministic license identification and provide more powerful way of expressing complex licensing rules.

SPDX requires only defining string like `SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 OR GPL-2.0-or-later` in the license (or readme) file. I think licensee could detect that string, parse it with an already existing SPDX tool and return licenses from this string with 100% certainty.

Thank you for considering it

title: Some way to filter auto-generated lines?
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

`@npmcli/template-oss` adds an auto-generated line that's not rendered when rendering the markdown license. In [this license](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/npm/gauge/main/LICENSE.md) for example, removing the line results in a match of 97.81% to the ISC license, compared to a match of 79.29% with the line included.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Some solution ideas:
1. Render the markdown and then convert the rendered markdown to plaintext before doing the comparison.
2. Strip markdown/html comments from markdown (a more targeted version of solution 1).
3. Add some way to explicitly filter matching lines from LICENSES (i.e. allow user-provided line filters).

Solution 1 is appealing because it might generally improve "cleaning" a license before comparison.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

You can lower the confidence_threshold, but going from 97 -> 79 is significant.

### Additional context

This particular auto-generated line seems to be somewhat common in popular npm packages.

title: Attribution with Multiple Copyright Markers with Indent Not Recognized
body:
### Describe the bug

The BSD-2 License TrueNAS uses on its own repository [here](https://github.com/truenas/py-libzfs/blob/a78d6fcf3c35368d43ec62311de0dfba1711c108/LICENSE) will not be correctly recognized.
Testing it with the newest version of licensee, I found out that its most probably the attribution as licensee correctly determines the license if each attribution becomes its own prefix (see below)

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. Save one of both example files below as LICENSE
2. Run licensee on it
    - e.g. `podman run --rm -v "$(pwd)":/repo licensee detect /repo` after building the image
3. See that the original is not recognized

### Expected behavior

If possible, it should recognize intended attributions and group them together.

### Example files

<details>

              2015-2017 Jakub Klama <jakub.klama@gmail.com>

title: "Ruby" license is not detected
body:
### Describe the bug

License "ruby" in a gemspec file is ignored.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. Make a gemspec file, `test/test.gemspec`:

    ```ruby
    Gem::Specification.new do |spec|
      spec.name          = "test"
      spec.version       = "0.0"
      spec.summary       = %q{Test}
      spec.description   = %q{Test for license}
      spec.license      = "Ruby"
    end
    ```

2. Install `licensee`.

3. Run `licensee detect`.

    ```shell-session
    $ licensee version
    9.16.0

    $ licensee detect .
    License:        NOASSERTION
    Matched files:  test.gemspec
    test.gemspec:
      Confidence:  90.00%
      Matcher:     Licensee::Matchers::Gemspec
      License:     NOASSERTION
    ```

### Expected behavior

"Ruby" license, which is in SPDX licenses list, is detected as the license.

While 4.0 is probably a better license, lots of media still is under "only" the 3.0 license. The result of checking on the official plain-text version of this license:

```text
License:        NOASSERTION
Matched files:  MEDIA_LICENSE.txt
MEDIA_LICENSE.txt:
  Content hash:  8d8899176a20aeadf20c80f9a6140ba54b50fcb0
  License:       NOASSERTION
  Closest non-matching licenses:
    CC-BY-SA-4.0 similarity:  34.54%
    GFDL-1.3 similarity:      32.98%
    ODbL-1.0 similarity:      32.60%
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'd like support for `cc-by-sa-3.0` support.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

### Additional context

The official licence text (in plain form): <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/legalcode.txt>

An example of a community with _tons_ of `cc-by-sa-3.0` media is the [Minetest](https://minetest.net) community.

title: Detect (proper) short Apache license
body:
Most Apache-licensed projects include the full text of the license. However, the license itself says:

> APPENDIX: How to apply the Apache License to your work.
> 
>       To apply the Apache License to your work, attach the following
>       boilerplate notice, with the fields enclosed by brackets "[]"
>       replaced with your own identifying information. (Don't include
>       the brackets!)  The text should be enclosed in the appropriate
>       comment syntax for the file format. We also recommend that a
>       file or class name and description of purpose be included on the
>       same "printed page" as the copyright notice for easier
>       identification within third-party archives.
> 
>    Copyright [yyyy] [name of copyright owner]
> 
>    Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
>    you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
>    You may obtain a copy of the License at
> 
>        http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
> 
>    Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
>    distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
>    WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
>    See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
>    limitations under the License.

`LICENSE` files with just this (filled-in) boilerplate don't appear to be detected as Apache, see e.g. https://github.com/shlevy/priodyn

title: Scan `LICENSES` folder (and maybe other common folders)
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

If you want to have multiple license files in your project, you may want to add them inside a subfolder.

The [REUSE](https://reuse.software) project which aims to make licensing more strict require the licenses files to be in a `LICENSES` folder in the project root folder.

I would like to follow REUSE recommendations and be able to run the associated linter, but if I put my licenses in a folder they are no longer recognized and displayed by GitHub which kinda defeat the point of having clear licensing.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Scan not only the root folder but also the `LICENSES` folder for licenses (and maybe other common folder names?).

Would be solved by https://github.com/licensee/licensee/issues/603, but this would have a bigger performance hit I guess.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

None

### Additional context

None

title: Overconfidence
body:
### Criteria for matching seems too low

I am using the [Living Licence 1.0.0](https://gitlab.com/living-code/living-license/-/blob/1fe5a9cd83d4d11c0f43e90fff11c4545665f187/living-license-1.0.0.md) for a project and the results I get from running this script are as follows:

1. 55.67% match with the Blue Oak Model 1.0.0 license with no modifications
2. 55.67% match with the Blue Oak Model 1.0.0 license when the markdown is not formatted as an active link
3. 57.97% match when the Blue Oak Model attribution text is removed entirely

These observations suggest to me that none of these results are sufficiently reliable matches to confidently assert the license as being the Blue Oak License 1.0.0 programmatically as is happening right now?

IT seems more sensible to use a more reliable criteria, perhaps a match of 95% or more perhaps?

### Steps to reproduce

1. Download the affected license <https://gitlab.com/living-code/living-license/-/raw/main/living-license-1.0.0.md?ref_type=heads&inline=false>
2. Run the `licensee detect` command
3. Remove the active link to the *Blue Oak* license
5. Repeat the `licensee detect` command
6. Remove the entire attribution line
7. Repeat the `licensee detect` command

### Expected resullts

I would not expect this package to assert an existing license when the match is only at a 50-60% confidence level. I would expect an 'unknown' or 'other' category when the confidence level is less than about 90-95% perhaps?

### Screenshots

![licensee-screenie](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/assets/83780461/21a42665-8c5d-49f0-a141-5488024d986b)
![screenie-2](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/assets/83780461/7384a9f0-688e-4f92-b106-fb76afb75d5b)

### Additional notes

The page <https://gitlab.com/living-code/living-source> INCORRECTLY shows this link:

This project is licensed under the Blue Oak Model License 1.0.0. [Learn more](https://blueoakcouncil.org/license/1.0.0)

The project is actually licensed under a derivative of that license NOT the Blue Oak License, which could have problematic legal effects for users of code licensed under this license.

title: Add CDDL-1.0
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you're trying to solve.

It appears that CDDL-1.0 is not detected. According to [OSI](https://opensource.org/license/cddl-1-0) it’s a “popular/strong community” license. It might be old but some people prefer it over the MPL and some well-known projects use it (e.g. ZFS, illumOS, cdrtools).

### Describe the solution you'd like

Is it enough to add https://github.com/spdx/license-list-data/blob/main/license-list-XML/CDDL-1.0.xml into the license folder? If so, I could create a PR that just does that. If there’s more to do, let me know.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

NA

### Additional context

Note that one can opt out of the automatic license version upgrade according to section 4.2 of the license. According to [SPDX expressions](https://spdx.github.io/spdx-spec/v3.0.1/annexes/spdx-license-expressions/#simple-license-expressions) one should use `CDDL-1.0+` when there is no opt-out and `CDDL-1.0` when there is. Since it’s hard to detect the opt-out (location can be outside of license and there is no mandated text), it makes sense to err towards the opt-out.

title: Feature request: Ignore presentational `<h1>`s on the first line of a LICENSE.md file
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe the problem you’re trying to solve.

I’m always frustrated when I want to make my `LICENSE.md` file more human‐readable by adding a simple `<h1>` title (e.g., `# MIT License`) on the very first line. Currently, adding any content, including a Markdown h1 heading, to the first line of a `LICENSE.md` file prevents GitHub’s Licensee tool from automatically recognizing the license.

This forces many mantainers to choose between having a clearly titled license file (for human readers) and benefiting from GitHub’s automated license recognition (which displays the license badge and information on the repository page).

### Describe the solution you’d like

I would like Licensee to be updated to **ignore or strip the Markdown `<h1>` prefix (`# `)** from the *very first line* of a `LICENSE.md` file before it attempts to match the file’s content against its database of known licenses.

This would allow users to add a simple, clear title to their `LICENSE.md` file like:

```markdown
# MIT License

Copyright © [year] [fullname]

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED “AS IS”, WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND,
EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES
OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND
NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT
HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY,
WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING
FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR
OTHER DEALINGS IN THE SOFTWARE.
```

And still have Licensee successfully identify the license as the MIT License.

### Describe alternatives you’ve considered

1. **Leaving the `<h1>` out:** This is the current common workaround. However, it makes the LICENSE.md file itself less clear and less visually organized when viewed directly, forcing users to rely solely on the GitHub UI badge or the README.md for license identification.
2. **Adding license information to README.md:** While good practice to mention the license in the README, it doesn’t resolve the issue of the LICENSE.md file being less self‐describing, nor does it enable the automatic license badge on GitHub’s UI for the LICENSE.md file itself.
3. **Licensee performing full Markdown parsing:** This would be overly complex and likely unnecessary for this specific problem. My suggestion is a much simpler, targeted preprocessing step.

### Additional context

- A top‐level heading on the first line is a purely presentational element and does not alter the legal substance of the license text itself.
- It’s highly unlikely that a legally binding license text would genuinely begin with a Markdown `<h1>` prefix as part of its substantive content.
- This small change to Licensee’s parsing logic could significantly improve the user experience and readability for countless open‐source projects without compromising the accuracy or robustness of license detection.

title: Consider adding support for the REUSE specification
body:
Hi, *licensee* only seems to scan a few top-level files, which makes me suspect (apologies if I'm wrong - I haven't read the code), that it results in determining a single license for a project. But licensing is much more complicated.

Luckily, there is a known way to precisely specify the licensing of a project, however complicated it gets. The *Debian* project has its `dep5` files, and the *REUSE* specification supports a `dep5` file (in the project's `.reuse` directory: `.reuse/dep5`), and it also makes use of the source files themselves for licensing information (in the form of *SPDX* comments and traditional copyright comments). The *REUSE* specification exists so that software can accurately determine the exact licensing of software for the purpose of automated compliance audits of third party software.

Please consider adding support for this. It seems like a good idea. I don't know how popular it is. I use it. *Github* doesn't make use of it. So maybe it's not important. But the *Linux* kernel (70%) and *KDE* use it. It's a European thing. Either way, I thought I should mention it just in case you weren't familiar with it. It seems like a perfect fit.

For more details, see: [REUSE - Make licensing easy for everyone](https://reuse.software/)

P.S. I haven't used *licensee*, so I have no actual issue with it. *Github* fed this project to me on my home page, and I came to have a look at it.

P.P.S. I'm not involved in the *REUSE* specification. I just use it.

title: Line break on Copyright holder kills license identification
body:
Using `licensee` directly, I was able to debug a [BSD3 license file](https://github.com/e2nIEE/pandapower/blob/197bc5b9678769b7e5eb22059e8140df9243ca39/LICENSE) that was not being correctly classified on GitHub.

It was caused by the copyright holder text being broken over two lines. 
I [opened a PR](https://github.com/e2nIEE/pandapower/pull/2914) to fix this but have been requested to upstream it as this line break shouldn't be the difference between a license being correctly identified or not.

### Expected behavior

The copyright holder text can be any number of lines long and it will all be considered separate to the main license text.

title: Markdown formatted version of CC-BY-4.0 not recognized
body:
I just added a markdown version of the CC-BY-4.0 license to my repository. I was surprised to see that it was not identified.

See https://github.com/SababaSec/ctf-writeups/blob/main/LICENSE.md

---

For comparison, I have a markdown version of the CC0-1.0 license on another repository, and that one works fine:

https://github.com/hb20007/awesome-gen-ai-fails/blob/main/LICENSE.md
